findings.description: Off-by-one: condition `len(hits) <= self.limit` allows limit+1 requests per window instead of limit; must be `< self.limit`.
`remaining` can be -1 when the off-by-one extra request is admitted, violating the implied non-negative contract for callers.
`current_usage()` accesses `self._hits[key]` on a defaultdict, silently creating an empty entry for every unseen key and inflating the dict, causing `sweep()` to count and remove phantom buckets.
`sweep()` removes only fully-expired buckets but never prunes stale timestamps within surviving buckets, so any active client that was once busy accumulates unbounded stale entries in memory.
`retry_after` can be zero or negative when `hits[0]` is within floating-point epsilon of the cutoff; callers receive a nonsensical negative wait time with no documented handling contract.
No thread-safety: `check()`, `reset()`, and `sweep()` mutate a shared `defaultdict` without any locking; concurrent WSGI/ASGI workers produce data races and can bypass the rate limit entirely.
Client key trusts the first entry of client-controlled `X-Forwarded-For`; any caller can spoof an arbitrary IP and evade per-client rate limiting — detail deferred to critic-security.
`hits.pop(0)` on a plain list is O(n); under high per-client volume the pruning loop degrades quadratically — should use `collections.deque` with `popleft()` — detail deferred to critic-performance.
